subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s degree
Completed undergraduate studies in English or Education, focusing on teaching methods and literature
Participated in student teaching as part of degree requirements
Attended professional development workshops for secondary education

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: AP Language Arts Teacher (11th grade)
Teaches advanced placement English Language Arts to high school juniors
Responsible for daily instruction, curriculum planning, and student assessment
Designs and evaluates essay-based assignments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the education sector
Employed in public secondary (high school) education
Part of a school district serving diverse student populations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works at a large public high school with approximately 1,500–2,000 students
Faculty consists of over 70 staff members
Environment is structured, with set schedules and established procedures

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Public secondary education, grades 9–12
Focuses on college preparatory and AP coursework
Serves a diverse, suburban student body

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 3–5 years of teaching experience in current role
Started teaching AP Language Arts shortly after graduation
Has completed at least three full academic years in the same position

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Early-career educator (entry to mid-level)
Not in a formal leadership or administrative role
Has developed expertise in AP curriculum but is not a department chair

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Teaches AP Language Arts classes to approximately 200 students daily
Designs and grades essay-based assignments
Uses Canvas and other platforms to post assignments and feedback
Leads class lectures, group activities, and discussions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 60% of the day teaching in the classroom
Allocates 30% of work hours to grading papers and assignments
Devotes 10% to planning lessons, meetings, and administrative tasks
Most grading is done outside of classroom instruction hours

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has moderate autonomy in designing lesson plans and classroom activities
Follows district and AP guidelines for curriculum and assessments
Can independently choose teaching methods and grading rubrics within set parameters
Consults with department leads for major curriculum changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Leading classroom discussions and lectures comes naturally
Quickly develops rapport with students and manages class routines
Efficiently sets up assignments on Canvas
Facilitating group activities is comfortable and engaging

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Grading large volumes of essays accurately and fairly is challenging
Providing individualized feedback for 200 students is time-consuming
Adapting lessons for students with varying skill levels requires extra effort
Staying current with AP standards and rubrics can be complex

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Manually grading essays for grammar and structure is repetitive
Entering grades into multiple systems is data-heavy and routine
Tracking assignment submissions and late work can be automated
Checking for plagiarism and formatting issues could be handled by AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys interacting with students and witnessing their progress
Finds lesson planning for creative writing units engaging
Views repetitive grading and administrative paperwork as tedious
Enjoys group activities and lively classroom debates

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Grading 200 essays takes significant personal time, often spilling into evenings
Switching between grading platforms can be inefficient
Tracking late submissions and missing work is cumbersome
Providing high-quality feedback under time constraints is challenging

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Enjoys tasks where student interaction is high, leading to higher energy and productivity
Is skilled at lesson delivery, making classroom time both effective and enjoyable
Productivity drops during large grading cycles due to repetitive workload
Feels most productive when balancing teaching with manageable grading loads

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally open to experimenting with new educational tech
Sees value in tools that save time or enhance teaching
Curious about AI and willing to try new platforms if relevant

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to updates in Canvas or grading software
Comfortable learning through self-guided tutorials or peer demonstrations
Can adjust teaching methods to incorporate digital resources

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to spend time on training if it leads to measurable time savings
Prefers short, focused workshops to lengthy courses
More likely to invest time if the tool is widely adopted by colleagues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential to reduce repetitive grading work
High workload during grading periods can limit time for tech adoption
Skeptical of tools that require extensive setup or don't integrate with existing systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers’ positive experiences with new tools increase willingness to try them
Department head recommendations carry significant weight
Observes adoption trends among colleagues before fully committing

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Canvas LMS daily for posting assignments, grading, and communication
Relies on Google Docs for collaborative writing and feedback
Accesses a district-provided grading system for official record-keeping

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Employs traditional red pens or digital annotation tools for essay marking
Utilizes physical textbooks and handouts for certain lessons
Uses email for parent and student communication

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient with Canvas, which is standard in many school districts
Experienced in using Google Workspace for Education
Has used Turnitin or similar plagiarism detection tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Canvas and district grading system do not always sync automatically
Occasionally must enter grades manually in multiple platforms
Lack of integration between Google Docs and some grading systems increases workload

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts like LLMs, prompt engineering, and AI-generated feedback
Knows that AI can assist with grammar checking and summary generation
Has a working knowledge of AI limitations and the need for human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT and similar LLM-based tools for drafting feedback or prompts
Has experimented with Grammarly or similar AI-based grammar checkers
Occasionally uses AI-assisted plagiarism detection tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs weekly to generate assignment prompts or model answers
Employs AI for grammar and style checks on student papers
Explores AI as a resource for lesson planning or idea generation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-generated essay feedback has sometimes highlighted overlooked grammar issues
Found LLMs occasionally misinterpret nuanced writing prompts
AI suggestions for lesson plans have provided time savings but require review for accuracy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal organizational training on AI tools currently provided
Relies on peer recommendations and online resources for AI tool adoption
Occasionally shares AI tool experiences with colleagues in informal settings

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers independent use of AI tools with the option to request step-by-step guidance if needed
Finds value in being able to review and edit AI-generated outputs before implementation
Appreciates flexibility in choosing interaction depth depending on task complexity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an assistant for routine or repetitive tasks
Sometimes uses AI as an advisor for brainstorming lesson ideas
Prefers to maintain final control over grading and feedback decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to adopting AI-driven grading assistance if accuracy and fairness can be ensured
Willing to experiment with AI for administrative or planning tasks
Prefers gradual integration of AI tools with clear benefits

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and feedback generation
Finds command-based or form-fill interfaces more suitable for batch processing tasks
Values clear instructions and transparent outputs in any interface

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Grading AP essays requires deep understanding of AP rubrics and writing conventions
Providing nuanced feedback often depends on knowledge of individual student progress
Lesson planning incorporates tacit awareness of class dynamics and culture
AI tools are less effective at handling subjective evaluation criteria

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for grammar and surface-level feedback but reviews all AI-generated grades
Skeptical of AI's ability to fully replace human judgment in essay evaluation
Uses AI suggestions as a starting point rather than final decision

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to automate repetitive grading components (grammar, structure)
Believes synthesis and final grades should remain human-led
Sees value in AI as a collaborator rather than a replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for administrative tasks (attendance, record-keeping)
Finds automation threatening if it removes personal judgment from essay grading
Requires clear override options and transparency for AI-graded submissions

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI can enhance efficiency but not replace core teaching skills
Views personal feedback and mentorship as irreplaceable by AI
Expects AI to reduce time spent on repetitive tasks, allowing focus on higher-level skills

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning to prompt and supervise AI-generated content is becoming important
Responsible for verifying the accuracy and fairness of AI outputs
Needs to communicate AI tool usage and limitations to students and parents

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Some concern about AI automating routine grading, but not core teaching
Does not fear full job replacement due to the human aspects of teaching
Monitors AI developments to stay relevant and adaptable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews AI-graded work before finalizing grades
Prefers tools that allow easy editing or override of AI suggestions
Insists on manual checks for critical assignments and assessments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: No formal school-wide policy on AI usage yet, but growing interest among staff
Colleagues increasingly discuss AI best practices in informal meetings
Expects future guidelines on AI use for grading and feedback

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as a tool for efficiency and innovation in education
Believes AI can help personalize learning at scale
Cautious about potential for bias or overreliance on automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes AI must be transparent and fair, especially in grading
Worries about data privacy and student information security
Supports ethical guidelines for AI use in education

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI tools that can provide context-aware feedback tailored to individual students
Wants seamless integration of AI grading tools with Canvas and other platforms
Would benefit from AI that can flag potential plagiarism or academic dishonesty more intelligently

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects grading and administrative work to become largely automated
Anticipates increased focus on coaching, mentorship, and individualized instruction
Believes teachers will need to become proficient in supervising and training AI systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Wants clear guidelines for responsible AI use in grading and feedback
Advocates for ongoing professional development in AI literacy
Encourages pilot programs to assess AI tool effectiveness before full rollout
